serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a portion of aloo gobi masala curry (approximately 350 g) and two pieces of freshly baked tandoori roti (approximately 200 g combined), providing roughly 750 kcal per set meal.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) and USDA FoodData Central — composite estimation for Aloo Gobi Masala with Tandoori Roti
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for the combined dish Aloo Gobi Masala with Tandoori Roti. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted-component method using ICMR-NIN data for potato curry, cauliflower curry, and tandoori roti components, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for individual ingredients (potato, cauliflower, whole-wheat flour, ghee, yogurt, spices). The input record values (energy 136.36 kcal, protein 3.64 g, fat 6.36 g, carbohydrate 16.36 g per 100 g) are consistent with this estimation and have been retained.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — potato curry and chapati/roti analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Aloo Gobi Masala with Tandoori Roti as a combined dish. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on: (1) boiled potato GI ~78 (University of Sydney GI Database), (2) chapati/whole-wheat roti GI ~52–62 (University of Sydney GI Database), (3) cauliflower and spices lower the overall glycaemic response. Weighted composite GI of approximately 65 is defensible for this mixed starchy-vegetable curry with roti. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (36.3 g from 550 g × 16.36 g/100 g) / 100 = 23.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 218 mg/100 g; a full 550 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, approaching 50–60% of the 2000 mg daily limit for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt during cooking, limit portion size to half a serving, and choose whole-wheat tandoori roti to benefit from the potassium content of the flour, which partially offsets sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~65) driven by high-GI potato and wheat-flour roti; a full 550 g serving delivers approximately 90 g carbohydrate with only 12 g dietary fibre, producing a glycemic load of ~23.6 which is clinically significant.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit the serving to half a set meal, request whole-wheat roti to increase fibre and reduce glycaemic response, and increase the cauliflower-to-potato ratio to lower the overall glycaemic load of the dish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 1.82 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.09 g/100 g are within acceptable limits; the dish is vegetarian, baked (roti), and contains beneficial spices including turmeric and ginger with anti-inflammatory properties.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume this dish in moderate portions; they should request ghee to be replaced with a small amount of olive or canola oil to further reduce saturated fat, and avoid adding extra salt or butter to the roti.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (potato, tomato, green chilli) may trigger joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals, though turmeric and ginger provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are nightshade-sensitive should substitute potato with sweet potato or chickpeas and replace tomato-based gravy with a coconut-milk base, while retaining cauliflower and the turmeric-ginger spice profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Gobi Masala with Tandoori Roti is a classic North Indian vegetarian set meal widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore. The dish consists of tender potato (aloo) cubes and cauliflower (gobi) florets slow-simmered in a rich, aromatic onion-tomato gravy seasoned with cumin, turmeric, coriander, garam masala, ginger, garlic, and green chilli, finished with a small amount of ghee and yogurt for body and flavour. It is served alongside two pieces of freshly baked tandoori roti — a soft, slightly charred whole-wheat flatbread cooked in a clay tandoor oven. The curry has a warm, earthy, mildly spiced flavour with a thick, clingy gravy; the roti provides a chewy, lightly smoky counterpoint. Nutritionally, the dish provides 136 kcal per 100 g, with 3.6 g protein, 6.4 g fat (1.8 g saturated), and 16.4 g carbohydrate with 2.2 g dietary fibre. A full 550 g set meal delivers approximately 750 kcal and 90 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium at 218 mg/100 g (approximately 1200 mg per full serving) warrants caution for hypertensive individuals. The dish is vegetarian, halal-suitable, and contains gluten and dairy allergens. Turmeric and ginger contribute anti-inflammatory curcumin and gingerol compounds. The dish is culturally significant in Indian cuisine and is a staple comfort food in the Indian diaspora community in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central component data for this composite dish; no single approved-source entry exists for Aloo Gobi Masala with Tandoori Roti as a combined preparation. The input values from the GrabFood-sourced record are consistent with the component-based estimation and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
